transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have a stomach ache.
When did your stomach ache start?
About 2 hours ago.
Can you describe where exactly in your stomach the pain is located?
It's kind of upper dull pain.
Any nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements?
No vomiting or bowel movements.
Any fever, chills, or sweating?
But I do feel nauseous.
Any history of similar stomach pain before or any known digestive issues?
No.
Any chest pain, shortness of breath, or sweating?
No.
Any recent eating or drinking that might have triggered the pain, such as spicy food, alcohol, or unusual meals?
I did go out for some pretty spicy Thai earlier today.
Any history of liver or gallbladder problems, such as gallstones or hepatitis?
No.
Any pain that worsens with eating or improves with antacids?
No.
Any history of alcohol use or recent use of medications like NSAIDs or aspirin?
Not today.
How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
No.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

It sounds like you’re experiencing stomach pain likely caused by irritation from spicy food, which is often called gastritis. Since I can’t examine you or run tests over the phone, a licensed physician will review your information and may recommend an antacid or other medication to help ease your symptoms. For now, you can try over-the-counter antacids after meals if you can take them safely. If your pain gets worse, or if you develop fever, vomiting, or notice black or bloody stools, please seek urgent in-person care right away. If you need an exam or testing, going to urgent care or the emergency room is the best option. A physician will review your case and decide if further treatment or testing is needed.
